title: Xbox's new VP of engineering quits just 2 months after taking the job
summary: Jared Palmer, who was picked to work directly with CEO Asha Sharma on the division's "most complex" problems, has taken a role at AI company Cognition.
body: Just two months after he was appointed the new vice president of engineering at Xbox and technical advisor to CEO Asha Sharma, Jared Palmer is now the former vice president of engineering at Xbox and technical advisor to CEO Asha Sharma. In an update to his LinkedIn profile , Palmer said he's joined AI company Cognition to serve as its vice president of engineering instead. "I’m grateful to Asha and everyone at Microsoft and Xbox for an amazing year," Palmer wrote in a very to-the-point missive. "Excited for this next chapter." "Cognition operates Devin, the first autonomous software engineer," the company's website says, in case you were wondering what it actually does. "Devin plans, writes, tests, and ships production code on its own, working inside your codebase and the tools your team already uses." AI is definitely Palmer's thing: Prior to his brief tenure at Xbox, he was vice president of product at Microsoft's CoreAI division, which Sharma headed up before taking the reins at Xbox. That was a fairly short spin too, lasting just eight months; before that he spent roughly four years as the vice president of AI at AI infrastructure company Vercel. Sharma said when Palmer was a…

title: Marathon's experimental PvE mode is live, immediately tripling its daily player count
summary: Cryo Archive is open for all, for a limited time.
body: Today, Bungie launched Marathon's 1.1.5 update, featuring its awaited Vault Breaker experimental PvE mode which offers a two-week window for players to contend with the typically-endgame Cryo Archive map and its many hazards and puzzles without any twitchy, murderous squads of competing players hanging around. Shortly after the Vault Breaker update went live, SteamDB charts showed that Marathon's concurrent player count had jumped to over 14,800 players. That's not a massive number compared to its peers on Steam's Most Played ranking , but it's roughly triple the activity that Marathon pulled at similar times of day throughout the last week—and the most concurrent player activity since player numbers started bleeding after the June free weekend. Vault Breaker trades Marathon's typical extraction loop for an alternative progression scheme. Unlike in normal excursions, runners in Vault Breaker won't keep any gear they pick up and extract with during a run. Instead, the goal is to loot Vault Data that can be used for persistent upgrades to your initial Vault Breaker kit or spent on gear that can be carried into standard runs, offering a more roguelite framework for Bungie's gunplay. …

title: For the first time, Unity demonstrates 'an Unreal character running through a Unity world'
summary: "Connect ALL the engines!" exclaims Epic CEO Tim Sweeney.
body: Unity has demonstrated a Unity Engine game running inside of an Unreal Engine game, marking a step in the direction of Epic Games CEO Tim Sweeney's vision for an interconnected gaming ecosystem. The demonstration took place this week at the Unite Seoul keynote, where Unity also announced the next version of its game engine. Unity's goal with the project, in partnership with Epic, is to allow Unity developers to publish their games in Fortnite. "You will see, for the first time, a Unity game rendering natively inside Unreal Engine," said Unity senior director of technical product management James Stone as he launched the demo. "Now this is not live in Fortnite yet, but it shows what's possible for a shared foundation. "What you're seeing here is a game simulated in Unity, rendered with Unreal. Now, that's not video streamed, there's no smoke and mirrors. This is all real time. But it's not just rendering. Watch the avatar. That's an Unreal character running through a Unity world. That's physics, lighting, input: all of it synchronized between engines." Last year we announced a partnership with Epic Games to bring Unity to Fortnite - and today, we want to show you where we are.This …

That influx of Chinese workers is part of a…

title: A hacker has reportedly wiped out parts of the land registry database of Romania, bringing its real-estate market to a shuddering halt
summary: Backups are said to be on the way.
body: A hacker going by the name of ByteToBreach has broken in to Romania's cadastre, or land registry, and reportedly wiped information from its databases. Sources speaking to Risky Business have told the outlet that the hacker entered the system using valid credentials, exfiltrated the data within, and later wiped info from its servers after a failed extortion attempt. ByteToBreach is reported to be the same hacker that breached the Swedish government's e-portal earlier this year, among other alleged acts of hackery. The stolen data was said to be announced for sale on two forums. In the meantime, the country's real-estate market appears to have been heavily disrupted . Romanian news outlet Jurnalul De Arges reports a post originating from notary Ana Stan that reads: "I am a notary. Since Tuesday I cannot issue a land registry extract, I cannot authenticate a sale, I cannot register a mortgage." While citizens and notaries are unable to obtain proof of ownership, new real-estate transactions have been brought to a shuddering halt. The disruption also falls mere weeks before the government's plans to raise the VAT on new homes to 21% from 9% come into effect, which means those hoping t…

title: It's probably time to update 7-Zip as the app has just been patched to fix a pretty big vulnerability
summary: It will need to be manually updated to version 26.02 or later.
body: If you use 7-Zip for your file compression and decompression you might want to update, as there's been a pretty big vulnerability patched. And yes, I mean manually update, as 7-Zip doesn't auto-update. You can download and run a new installer from the 7-Zip website . If you're updated to version 26.02 (or later, if you're reading this in the future) then you'll be fine. To check this, go to Help > About 7-Zip in the app. The vulnerability, as explained by Zero Day Initiative (via TechSpot ), "allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of 7-Zip. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file. "The specific flaw exists within the processing of XZ chunked data. Crafted XZ-compressed data can trigger an overflow of a heap-based buffer. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current process." It sounds like you'd have to try to use a compromised compressed file in 7-Zip for the exploit to work. This might initially sound like a bit of a nothing burger because compromised files can obviously compromise your PC (duh). But I'd say …

title: TSMC admits it's 'jealous' of memory chipmakers' margins, but is limiting its own price hike because its customers would not survive price increases of 'four to five times'
summary: The company announces a 10 - 15% price increase, but isn't keen to emulate the memory industry's runaway pricing.
body: You can be sure that whatever TSMC does next, the rest of the industry will follow. Unfortunately, it looks like this major player in chip manufacturing is set to raise its prices by as much as 10% in 2027. This is due to both the rising cost of materials as well as the company's overseas fab construction projects (such as the already booked up though not even built yet 2nm fab in Arizona ). Price increases ranging between 5 to 10% will affect various chip production services offered by TSMC (via Nikkei Asia ). Sources also claim that extra orders of high-performance computing chips not otherwise accounted for in customers' original forecasts will incur a 10 to 15% premium on top. The services affected include TSMC's 7nm and more advanced fabrication processes. As TSMC accounts for over 70% of the global semiconductor foundry market , Big Tech will have little choice but to pay the bill when it's due. Waiting until the start of 2027 to raise its prices is considered a less aggressive move within the industry (especially in light of memory prices being predicted to rise by as much as 50% in Q3 of 2026). Indeed, we've already seen more than our fair share of price increases this yea…

title: GM announces new gas-powered Cadillac vehicles amid EV pullback

title: Acer's Predator Atlas 8 pricing puts the nail in the handheld coffin for me: it is now a luxury market
summary: Just as predicted.
body: A couple of months ago, Intel announced a couple of new chips for handheld gaming PCs, the Intel Arc G3 and G3 Extreme. It was certainly exciting to have something new on that front, given we've been seeing mostly the same silicon crammed inside such devices for a very long time. But at the time I warned that signs pointed towards yet more prohibitively expensive price tags . And now we're seeing Acer's pricing for its new Predator Atlas 8 handhelds, it's safe to say I was unfortunately right. As spotted by KitGuru and confirmed by yours truly , the UK pricing for these handhelds, containing new Intel Panther Lake chips and expected to launch in October this year, ranges from £1,000 to £1,500. The cheapest has a regular G3 chip, 16 GB of memory, and a 512 GB SSD. You can up the memory to 24 GB for another £100. A G3 Extreme version with a 1 TB SSD and 24 GB of memory will be able to be nabbed for £1,300, and bumping that up to 32 GB of RAM will get you up to the top £1,500 price tag. Converting those figures to dollars, that makes for a handheld that ranges from (roughly) $1,300 to $2,000. Though of course prices don't usually track exchange rates exactly. Yep, that's pretty expen…

title: Valve has replaced a Steam Machine for free after it was stolen by porch pirates, saving one UK redditor roughly £1,000
summary: A feel-good story? What year is this again?
body: The Steam Machine is a pricey piece of hardware. Which is why UK redditor mdumtshali must have been heartbroken (or very annoyed) when theirs was stolen from their doorstep after the Royal Mail dropped it off while they were at work. The delivery driver sent a picture of the Steam Machine box to prove it had been delivered, and even tried to cover it with a plastic bag (via Notebookcheck ). But alas, when mdumtshali returned home, porch pirates (my new favourite term) had already absconded with the ill-fated mini PC. However, mdumtshali says they eventually contacted Steam Support, which duly agreed to send out a replacement Steam Machine free of charge. "Honestly, kudos to Steam Support," says the redditor. "[I] thought I was going to be out £1000, which I really can't afford." Steam Support initially asked mdumtshali to check with neighbours to see if the parcel had been taken in, which they duly did to no avail. As the Steam Machine is a high-value item, it's surprising that a signature wasn't required by the Royal Mail for delivery confirmation, which would have likely prevented this whole fiasco in the first place. Royal Mail Left My Steam Machine Outside from r/steammachine …

title: Ex-TSMC manager first to be indicted under National Security Act for allegedly attempting to leak core Taiwanese chipmaking technologies to China
summary: They could face up to seven years in prison.
body: Taiwanese prosecutors have reportedly indicted a former TSMC manager for allegedly stealing trade secrets to give to China. This case, according to a spokesperson from Taiwan's High Prosecutors Office who relayed the information to Nikkei Asia , is the first of its kind: "Although the attempt to transfer the technology to China was unsuccessful, the case remains highly significant as the first prosecution under the National Security Act involving an alleged attempt to leak Taiwan's national core technologies to China." The plan was, says Nikkei Asia, "to establish a semiconductor materials analysis company in China called CSMAC." Apparently, the prosecutors discovered that Chen, a former TSMC deputy manager, worked with another Hong Kong man to steal TSMC trade secrets, copying 21 confidential documents, taking them home to study for use in China. The prosecutors also claim that Chen was considering recruiting more talent from Taiwan for CSMAC. Chen has been detained for the past couple of months, and could face up to seven years in prison. TSMC, on the other hand, is apparently doing fine, as it has recovered all the documents. This time last year I reported that the Taiwanese ch…

title: I'm very into this Papers, Please-esque puzzle game about identifying traitors to the socialist motherland
summary: Hey, it's a living.
body: Citizens! We live in times of unprecedented danger! We are beset on all sides by traitors and saboteurs. Reactionaries whisper treason in dimly lit rooms. Their mouths spew poison and lies that eat away at the foundations of the worker-peasant revolution, at the root of the world revolution itself. And, worst of all, no one gave me a desk big enough to deal with it. (Image credit: JKBC) What Awaits, Aleksey? is a clever mystery/puzzle game that caters to all my specific obsessions. It's a Stasi simulator, more or less—you are Aleksey Lutomski, a recent hire at the security service of a fictional 20th-century communist state, tasked with identifying the members of an underground cell of dissidents who are going around saying mean things about the motherland. For the most part, this means obsessing over the tidiness of your desk. It is no longer World War 2 and you are not Stierlitz . You are not kicking in doors and taking down fascist spies with a Tokarev. It is 1970: the socialist sixth of the Earth has settled into bleary stagnation. The enemies of the state are disaffected academics and you fight them by going through their mail. So, each day, you sit behind your desk and open …

title: Nvidia finally reveals what controls devs actually have for DLSS 5, and don't worry folks, there are TWO main sliders to control the AI filter
summary: The company is, however, 'building more' controls.
body: Nvidia announced DLSS 5 back in March and it received, let's say, mixed reactions . A lot of people weren't happy about how, judging by the demos , it seemed to essentially just slap a 'yassifying' AI filter on top of a game scene. Nvidia itself confirmed that all the tech has to work with for each AI frame is a traditional 2D frame and motion vectors, essentially confirming the "filter" idea. Now, at SIGGRAPH 2026, the company has explained that developers will get two main sliders to control DLSS 5's intensity. My initial reaction was probably the same as yours: two whole sliders, whoop-de-doo. It's hardly a massive improvement from the one on-to-off masking slider we previously thought devs would have access to. But let's take a look at what they're actually controlling and how it works before we jump to conclusions. The two sliders are for 'structural intensity' and 'tone intensity'. Despite what the word "structure" might seem to imply, Nvidia is keen to point out that "it is not changing the geometry in any way, and it is uplifting the image." The structural intensity slider in fact is "modulating the high-frequency detail of the frame." This includes things such as ambient …

title: National Grid taps into US AI power boom
summary: The UK utility company is investing $1.75bn in a developer focused on supplying power to data centres

title: A new Elder Scrolls is still years away, but this fun Arthurian take on the formula is 50% off right now
summary: Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon is a very competent Bethesda RPG, not made by Bethesda.
body: It's a little surprising there aren't more The Elder Scrolls clones. Aside from Avowed, the only other example I can think of is Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon. It launched in May last year after a stint in early access, and the response was positive, perhaps surprisingly so, considering how tough it must be for a relatively young studio to make a game of this scale. Our reviewer wrote that it "puts an interesting spin on Arthurian legend and presents a competent world that plays like a love letter to the open-world first-person RPG genre." It's a "dark reimagination" of Arthurian legends, set entirely on the island of Avalon. There are three distinct zones to explore—in this sense, it wends closer to Avowed—and it has all the usual distractions: you can fish, farm, cook, mine, smith weapons, mix spells, and sketch in your journal, between the usual bouts of dungeon crawling, overworld exploration, and "morally ambiguous" choice making. I've played a couple of hours on Steam Deck and it runs pretty well there too, albeit at around the 30-45fps mark. The art style is darker than Skyrim's, coming off almost as a hybrid of Dark Souls and Morrowind. It feels unmistakably like a fir…

title: Jennifer English, star of Baldur's Gate 3 and Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, leaves Tides of Annihilation lead role
summary: English, who won a BAFTA award for her work on Clair Obscur in 2026, says the decision was difficult but "personally necessary."
body: Jennifer English, who gained fame for her portrayal of Shadowheart in Baldur's Gate 3 and then won a BAFTA award for best performer in a leading role for Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, has announced that she is withdrawing from the role of Gwendolyn in the upcoming Tides of Annihilation in order to "properly look after [her] mental and physical health." Revealed in 2025, Tides of Annihilation struck me as an Arthurian Elden Ring set in modern-day London : Gwendolyn, the only survivor of the "Outworld invasion" that's shattered London, can summon the spirits of the Knights of the Round Table to help her do battle with the "demi-gods of Avalon." As videogames go, I'd say it sure looks very videogame-y . No points for originality there, but it's among the 50 most wishlisted games on Steam , and that's not nothing. But the game suffered a blow when developer Eclipse Glow Games announced that English was stepping down. "Earlier in June, Jennifer informed us that she would no longer be able to continue in the role," the studio wrote on X . "While we were, of course, saddened by her decision, we completely understood and respected Jennifer in prioritizing her well-being. We are also deeply…

title: Steam's Train Fest sale means all 790 pieces of Train Simulator Classic DLC can be yours for a mere $6,993 instead of $15,621
summary: Choo-choo-choose savings!
body: Steam's Train Fest has begun, meaning there are deals and discounts on lots of locomotive games, even a few brand new ones like Denshattack , which just came out this month. But when it comes to train sales there's really only one train game to talk about: Train Simulator Classic, a sim notable for having a whole heck of a lot of DLC: a whopping 790 pieces of it. And almost all of them are on sale this week. The base game itself has a hefty discount of 80% off , making it a steal at only $6 during Train Fest. And just about all of that tasty DLC is also on sale for anywhere between 20-80% off as well. I won't lie, it was trickier than I expected to figure out what the total discount on all that DLC adds up to. Perhaps for obvious reasons, Steam won't let me add all 790 pieces of DLC to my cart at once, but it also won't just display all of the pieces of DLC in a nice tallyable column, truncating the list to a mere 200 entries. Even the ever-useful Augmented Steam extension won't let me fill my cart with every piece of DLC in one fell swoop. Come on, storefronts, let me accidentally purchase thousands of dollars of software! But after a lot of clicking and scrolling and digging and…

title: 15 years before Dishonored, Thief devs considered letting Garrett shrink himself rat-sized so he could scurry through drains and mouse holes
summary: The idea was ultimately dropped, for good reasons.
body: Of the many remarkable abilities possessed by Dishonored hero Corvo Attano, perhaps the most remarkable is, well, possession: Corvo can possess creatures, most famously the game's plentiful rats, to get up to all sorts of trouble, including accessing locations that would be inaccessible to his full-sized body. As it turns out, this wasn't an entirely original idea. More than a decade earlier it had been considered for use in Dishonored's masterpiece progenitor, Thief: The Dark Project . Thief basically is Dishonored, speaking very broadly: A guy who's good at sneaking around gets caught up in a bunch of political nonsense he'd really rather avoid because of a woman he can't admit to being in love with because it'd be weird . One chief difference is that Garrett, the titular thief of the Thief games (the ones that matter, anyway) doesn't have any powers. He almost did, though. In a 2018 PC Gamer magazine interview marking the 20 th anniversary of Thief's debut, lead designer Tim Stellmach said Looking Glass Studios had pondered giving Garrett the ability to shrink himself and travel through small passageways like drains. The idea was ultimately dropped, though, for eminently practi…

title: 'There are limits to how much prices can be raised' says SK hynix chairman about memory crisis but I'm not sure the market will care
summary: Will more supply be enough?
body: The PC hardware market right now is not a pretty sight, so it's nice to hear one of the companies arguably at least in part responsible acknowledging how bad things are from a customer's perspective. Even if that is taken with the giant pinch of salt considering it's being approached from a business perspective, and discussing the ability for other companies to absorb costs. As The Chosun Daily reports , SK hynix group chairman (ie, head of the board) Chey Tae-won recently admitted to the the press that memory prices are "abnormally high" and said "there are limits to how much prices can be raised". Apparently the company is considering building a factory in the US to help with supply and trade pressures. "Supply must be increased to lower prices, even for the sake of semiconductor companies... Memory prices are currently at an abnormally high level. While AI companies can absorb increased costs through investments, PC and smartphone manufacturers have no choice but to pass semiconductor price hikes onto product prices." "Since most customers for these products are individuals, there are limits to how much prices can be raised. To prevent 'chipflation'—where rising chip prices dri…

title: 'With the power of illustration, we can capture the true, playful vibe of a kangaroo': Google explains the thinking behind its new 3D emojis
body: I like to imagine that, in thousands of years time, an alien species will land on the desert wasteland that was once Earth and begin combing through our digital records. What they'll likely discover fairly quickly is the rise of emojis in our daily communications during the late 2000s , a process Google is looking to push further into the modern age with proper 3D models. The Noto 3D collection of emojis was released earlier this year , but Google has gone into the design process (and a brief history of the form) in-depth in a blog post for World Emoji Day . No, I didn't know that was a thing either. "We don't just 'laugh' anymore," says Google. "We collapse, or internally scream. Modern internet culture has steadily moved from mild expressions to drama, hyperbole and overwhelm." "For years, 'face with tears of joy' (😂) was the undisputed emoji king, according to Gboard Federated Analytics. But following a multi-year decline, it slipped down the charts by 2025," the post continues. "Why? Because 😭 is a masterclass in modern vocabulary: It’s hilarious, it’s devastating and it captures absolute overwhelm." (Image credit: Darnexx on Nexus Mods) Uhh, sure thing. To be fair, 😂 often…

title: One of Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's leather jackets has fetched an auction price of $960,000: 'It has become a symbol for the vision at the forefront of the AI revolution'
summary: And now I have it I'm going to frame it and stick it up on my wall.
body: Steve Jobs had his turtlenecks, Jensen Huang has his iconic leather jackets. Well, one less of them now, anyway. Famous auctioneer Sotheby's put one of Huang's jackets up for auction over the weekend, with estimates placing the value between $40,000 to $60,000. However, the hammer price (after 65 bids) ended up being an astonishing $960,000—with the proceeds going towards young people's futures (via Tom's Hardware ). The auction was organized by Long Journey Ventures to benefit the Edge Institute , a non-profit that "convenes people working at the frontiers of tech, science, culture, and society in popup villages to live together and experiment towards a brighter future." "The proceeds will fund fellowships, grants, and residencies for the next generation of young builders to pursue their passions and projects, supporting them to find their higher purpose," says the Sotheby's page. But blimey, stone the crows etc, that's a lot of money for a leather jacket. Particularly as it's a Tom Ford model that usually retails for much less. Still, this has been worn and signed by the Nvidia CEO, and the auction listing has some wonderfully flowery copy to giggle at. (Image credit: Lex Fridma…

title: My sluggish limbs mean I'm terrible at Black Myth: Wukong on both a controller, as well as mouse and keyboard—so surely a brain-computer interface is the answer
summary: Thinking 'Get good' and making it so?
body: Translating brain activity into game inputs is the stuff of absurdly long-running sci-fi anime. It's also a tech that, though very much in its infancy, now exists in the real world too. For instance, attendees at this year's World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai could have a go at playing Black Myth: Wukong with just their brain waves. AI enthusiast Huang Lu shared a video of the non-invasive brain-computer interface at WAIC 2026 via X . According to the exhibit's staff, it only takes about five minutes to calibrate for each gamer. A WAIC press release describing the exhibit explains, "The non-invasive system captures and decodes neural signals, then converts the user's intentions into device commands. Related technology is also being used in sleep-related applications, early warning for epileptic seizures, and anesthesia monitoring." The setup here is a bit different to similar brain-computer interfaces I've seen. Namely, it doesn't require the user to think of very specific stimulus in order to activate different regions of the brain, a la streamer PerriKaryal's EmotivBCI setup . Instead of, say, thinking about a cricket jumping in order to dodge in-game, the setu…
